gcloud run services update popcre-core-sandbox --project lithe-breaker-323913 --region us-east4 --add-custom-audiences=https://api.core.sandbox.designflow.app
gcloud run services update popcre-item-sandbox --project lithe-breaker-323913 --region us-east4 --add-custom-audiences=https://api.item.sandbox.designflow.app
gcloud run services update popcre-tracking-sandbox --project lithe-breaker-323913 --region us-east4 --add-custom-audiences=https://api.tracking.sandbox.designflow.app
gcloud run services update popcre-sync-sandbox --project lithe-breaker-323913 --region us-east4 --add-custom-audiences=https://api.sync.sandbox.designflow.app"

do you understand what's going on? you can use gcloud SDK CLI authenticated on this machine to inspect the environment if you need.

(Tools: Glob, Grep, Read, WebFetch, WebSearch)

- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. It reads excerpts rather than whole files, so it locates code; it doesn't review or audit it. Specify search breadth: "medium" for moderate exploration, "very thorough" for multiple locations and naming conventions. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)

- general-purpose: General-purpose agent for researching complex questions, searching for code, and executing multi-step tasks. When you are searching for a keyword or file and are not confident that you will find the right match in the first few tries use this agent to perform the search for you. (Tools: *)

- Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. Returns step-by-step plans, identifies critical files, and considers architectural trade-offs. (Tools: All tools except Agent, ExitPlanMode, Edit, Write, NotebookEdit)
- statusline-setup: Use this agent to configure the user's Claude Code status line setting. (Tools: Read, Edit)

## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.

## devops-mcp
This DevOps MCP intentionally exposes a small always-on MCP tool surface:
health, list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, and invoke_tool.

For every host, Docker, systemd, file, or audit-log task, call tool_search first
to find the right hidden operation. Then call invoke_tool with the exact operation
name and an args object matching the operation description. Do not assume direct
tools such as run_command, docker_ps, read_file, or service_status are listed in
tools/list; they are discoverable operations to keep client context small.

Call list_capabilities to browse by category or safety class. Call
get_capability_details for one operation's full contract, including args and
examples. Call health first when you need server context, visible tools,
registered agents, or the complete operation-name list. Treat write-capable
operations with care: this server has root-level host access, so prefer
inspection commands before state-changing commands.

## recall-ai
Recall.ai lets developers send meeting bots to video calls, capture meeting data, and connect bots to calendar events. This MCP server provides read-only tools scoped to a Recall.ai workspace. A workspace is the Recall.ai account context that bots, calendars, docs defaults, and logs are read from. By default, the MCP uses the workspace selected when the user authorized the connection. If the account has access to multiple workspaces, workspace-scoped tools accept an optional workspace_id so the agent can read from a different workspace for that call. Use get_info to see the current workspace and API version. When the user names a workspace, call list_workspaces, match the name from the returned list, then pass the returned workspace ID to bot, calendar, log, and docs tools. Do not pass a workspace name as workspace_id. Use bot tools to list bots, inspect bot details and lifecycle events, and page through bot logs when debugging join, recording, or meeting behavior. Use list_calendars/get_calendar to inspect connected and disconnected calendar integrations, platform emails, credential status, and calendar connection/sync state. Use calendar event tools to see which bots were scheduled from calendar events. Use log tools for workspace-level API, calendar connection, recording, transcript, webhook, and integration errors when a bot-specific log query is too narrow. Use list_webhook_endpoints and list_webhook_deliveries when debugging dashboard webhook subscriptions, missing delivery attempts, destination responses, or a resource-specific webhook event such as a bot, recording, transcript, or artifact event. Use docs tools to search and fetch Recall.ai guides and API reference material for the current workspace's API version. Use search_docs when attempting to learn docs slugs, unlessthe slug came from list_docs, a Recall guide resource, or an explicit user-provided docs URL/slug. When drafting user-facing answers or understanding bot behavior, use docs tools for product behavior, API defaults, configuration fields, … [truncated]

## synology-monitor
This NAS MCP intentionally exposes only a small always-on tool surface:
list_capabilities, get_capability_details, tool_search, invoke_tool, run_command,
check_disk_space, and restart_nas_api.

For any NAS diagnostic, troubleshooting, recovery, storage, Drive/ShareSync,
backup, package, log, filesystem, snapshot, permission, or admin task, call
tool_search first unless the task is exactly disk-space checking, free-form
read-only shell diagnosis, or restarting nas-api. Most NAS capabilities are
hidden from tools/list by design to keep the AI session context small.

list_capabilities browses by group and safety class without invoking anything.
get_capability_details returns one operation's full contract. tool_search returns
clear operation descriptions plus the exact invoke_tool call shape. After choosing
a capability, call invoke_tool with the exact returned name, target, and args. Use
target "edgesynology1", "edgesynology2", or "both".

Write tools require a preview first. To execute after reviewing the preview,
call invoke_tool again with confirmed: true inside args. Do not invent or expose
bearer tokens.

## trigger
Trigger.dev MCP server to automate your Trigger.dev projects and answer questions about Trigger.dev by searching the docs. 
If you need help setting up Trigger.dev in your project please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup. 
If the user asks for help with adding Trigger.dev to their project, please refer to https://trigger.dev/docs/manual-setup.

## Windows-MCP

Windows MCP server provides tools to interact directly with the Windows desktop,
thus enabling to operate the desktop on the user's behalf.

- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). - simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.

content.text:
Yes — I understand what's going on, and I inspected the live environment to confirm it. **Your developer's diagnosis is aimed at the wrong thing.** Here's the actual picture.

## What's actually breaking

When you log in, your browser calls the BFF (backend-for-frontend) at `api.alsand.designflow.app/api/core/auth/azure`. That route is a **proxy** — the BFF turns around and calls the *core* service at the URL in its `CORE_BACKEND_URL` env var. I read that value off the live service:

```
CORE_BACKEND_URL    = https://api.core.alsand.designflow.app
ITEM_MASTER_...     = https://api.item.alsand.designflow.app
TRACKING_...        = https://api.track.alsand.designflow.app
DATA_SYNC_...       = https://api.sync.alsand.designflow.app
```

I then hit each of those backend domains directly:

| Domain the BFF calls | Result | Cloud Run domain mapping exists? |
|---|---|---|
| `api.core.alsand.designflow.app` | **525** (Cloudflare can't TLS-handshake the origin) | ❌ **missing** |
| `api.item.alsand.designflow.app` | **525** | ❌ **missing** |
| `api.sync.alsand.designflow.app` | **525** | ❌ **missing** |
| `api.track.alsand.designflow.app` | 403 (reaches Cloud Run, auth-gated — normal) | ✅ present → `popcre-albert-tracking-sandbox` |

The domain-mapping list in `us-east4` for this tenant only contains `alsand`, `api.alsand`, and **`api.track.alsand`**. The mappings for **core, item, and sync were never recreated after the Ohio→Virginia move.** Only tracking got redone.

So: DNS for `api.core.alsand…` still resolves (Cloudflare record), Cloudflare tries to reach the Google origin, but there's no Cloud Run domain mapping → no origin cert for that hostname → **TLS handshake fails → 525**. The BFF gets that 525, the request dies, and your browser shows it as "HTTP status 0 / cannot reach the server." That's your error, exactly.

## Why the developer's fix won't work

```
gcloud run services update popcre-core-sandbox ... --add-custom-audiences=https://api.core.sandbox.designflow.app
```

Two problems:
1. **Wrong hostnames.** That's the *generic* sandbox domain (`api.core.sandbox.designflow.app`). Your alsand BFF doesn't call those — it calls the **per-tenant** `api.core.alsand.designflow.app`. (It also targets `popcre-core-sandbox`, the generic service, not `popcre-albert-core-sandbox`, the one alsand actually uses.)
2. **Wrong layer.** Custom audiences only matter *after* a request reaches a Cloud Run service, for validating the auth token's `aud` claim. Right now the request can't reach Cloud Run at all (525 = no origin). Adding audiences fixes nothing until the mapping exists. And for what it's worth, **none** of your working services — prod, sandbox, or alsand — have any custom audiences set today, so it's likely not even the auth mechanism in play here.

## The correct fix

Recreate the three missing backend domain mappings (mirroring how `track` was already done):

```bash
gcloud beta run domain-mappings create --domain api.core.alsand.designflow.app \
  --service popcre-albert-core-sandbox --project lithe-breaker-323913 --region us-east4

gcloud beta run domain-mappings create --domain api.item.alsand.designflow.app \
  --service popcre-albert-item-sandbox --project lithe-breaker-323913 --region us-east4

gcloud beta run domain-mappings create --domain api.sync.alsand.designflow.app \
  --service popcre-albert-sync-sandbox --project lithe-breaker-323913 --region us-east4
```

After each is created, Cloud Run provisions a managed cert (the 525s stop once `CertificateProvisioned` flips to True — usually minutes, since DNS already points the right way via Cloudflare). Then SSO should complete.

One caveat to watch *after* the mappings are live: if the BFF→backend calls then start returning a clean **401/403** (instead of 525), *that's* when a custom-audiences (or IAM invoker) tweak would be the right next step — but with the correct `*.alsand.*` hostnames and the `popcre-albert-*` services. Given the working services run with no custom audiences, I don't expect it, but it's the only scenario where your developer's idea becomes relevant.

Want me to create the three domain mappings now, or would you rather hand the corrected commands to your developer?

Here's the verdict: **the mappings themselves are right — you just caught them mid-provisioning.**

What's confirmed good:
- All three new mappings point to the correct services (`api.core.alsand`→`popcre-albert-core-sandbox`, `item`→`item`, `sync`→`sync`). ✓
- Domains are routable (`DomainRoutable: True`). ✓

Why they're still 525: the **managed TLS certificates haven't been issued yet** — status is `CertificateProvisioned: Unknown / "Certificate issuance pending"`, with Google retrying every 5 minutes. Until the cert lands, Cloudflare can't complete the TLS handshake to the origin, so you get 525. That's expected for mappings created minutes ago, not a misconfiguration.

The DNS check is reassuring: your already-working `api.track.alsand` is in the **identical** proxied-through-Cloudflare state as the three new ones. Since track provisioned fine that way, the path is proven — these three just need to finish.

**What to do:** give it ~15–30 minutes and the 525s should turn into 403s (403 = reaching Cloud Run, auth-gated, which is the *healthy* state — that's what track shows). Then retry the SSO login.

**If they're still pending after ~30 min:** that "you must configure your DNS records for issuance to begin" message means Google's validation isn't completing through Cloudflare's proxy. The fix then is to temporarily set those three records to **DNS-only (grey cloud)** in Cloudflare so the cert can issue, then flip them back to proxied — but try waiting first, since track managed it while proxied.

Want me to keep an eye on it and ping you when the certs go live (or flag if they stall)? I can poll the three mappings on an interval.

############## BFF cloudbuild ##############
# BFF Cloud Run deploy. Trigger must supply substitutions (see cloudbuild-triggers.sh).
# Sandbox: _APP_ENV=sandbox, _API_ADDRESS=https://api.sandbox.designflow.app, backend URLs = each private Cloud Run HTTPS URL.
steps:
  # 📦 Build the image using Dockerfile
  - id: Build the image
    name: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker
    args:
      - build
      - -t
      - ${_IMAGE}
      - --build-arg=NODE_ENV=${_APP_ENV}
      - .

  # 🔃 Push the built image to artifact registry
  - id: Push the image to artifact registry
    name: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker
    args:
      - push
      - ${_IMAGE}

  # Env for Cloud Run: YAML file so commas in CORS_ORIGINS are safe (unlike --set-env-vars). Substitutions are expanded by Cloud Build before bash runs.
  - id: Create Cloud Run env file
    name: 'gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk'
    entrypoint: bash
    args:
      - -c
      - |
        cat <<ENVEOF >/workspace/run-env.yaml
        NODE_ENV: "${_APP_ENV}"
        API_ADDRESS: "${_API_ADDRESS}"
        CORS_ORIGINS: "${_CORS_ORIGINS}"
        CORE_BACKEND_URL: "${_CORE_BACKEND_URL}"
        ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL: "${_ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL}"
        TRACKING_BACKEND_URL: "${_TRACKING_BACKEND_URL}"
        DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL: "${_DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL}"
        API_KEY_JWT_TTL: "${_API_KEY_JWT_TTL}"
        ENVEOF

  - id: Deploy the image to cloud run
    name: 'gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk'
    entrypoint: gcloud
    args:
      - run
      - deploy
      - ${_SERVICE_NAME}
      - --image=${_IMAGE}
      - --region=${_REGION}
      - --port=${_PORT}
      - --concurrency=${_CONCURRENCY}
      - --max-instances=${_MAX_INSTANCES}
      - --allow-unauthenticated
      - --platform=${_PLATFORM}
      - --service-account=${_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME}
      - --env-vars-file=/workspace/run-env.yaml
      - |-
        --set-secrets=
        JWT_PRIVATE_KEY=projects/$PROJECT_NUMBER/secrets/JWT_PRIVATE_KEY/versions/latest,
        DESIGNFLOW_API_KEYS=projects/$PROJECT_NUMBER/secrets/DESIGNFLOW_API_KEYS/versions/latest

substitutions:
  _REGION: $$_REGION
  _SERVICE_NAME: $$_SERVICE_NAME
  _APP_ENV: $$_APP_ENV
  _APP_NAME: $$_APP_NAME
  _ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME: $$_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME
  _SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME: $$_SERVICE_ACCOUNT_NAME
  _PORT: $$_PORT
  _CONCURRENCY: $$_CONCURRENCY
  _MAX_INSTANCES: $$_MAX_INSTANCES
  _PLATFORM: $$_PLATFORM
  _CORE_BACKEND_URL: $$_CORE_BACKEND_URL
  _ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL: $$_ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL
  _TRACKING_BACKEND_URL: $$_TRACKING_BACKEND_URL
  _DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL: $$_DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL
  _API_ADDRESS: $$_API_ADDRESS
  _CORS_ORIGINS: $$_CORS_ORIGINS
  _API_KEY_JWT_TTL: 1h
  _IMAGE: ${_REGION}-docker.pkg.dev/${PROJECT_ID}/${_ARTIFACT_REPO_NAME}/${_APP_ENV}/${_APP_NAME}:${BUILD_ID}-${COMMIT_SHA}

options:
  logging: CLOUD_LOGGING_ONLY

images:
  - ${_IMAGE}

############## BACKEND (core) cloudbuild ##############
steps:
# Unit tests run in CI before deploy — not at container startup (Jest can hang on open DB handles in Cloud Run).
- id: Run unit tests
  name: node:lts-alpine

    - .

# 🔃 Push the built image to artifact registry

  name: gcr.io/cloud-builders/docker
  args:
    - push
    - ${_IMAGE}

# 🚀 Deploy the image to Google Cloud Run
- id: Deploy the image to cloud run
  name: 'gcr.io/google.com/cloudsdktool/cloud-sdk'
  entrypoint: gcloud

    - --image=${_IMAGE}
    - --region=${_REGION}

    - |-

1	# AGENTS.md — Designflow BFF: Developer Guide
2	
3	Read this before touching any code. It is the authoritative guide for this repository.
4	
5	---
6	
7	## 🚨 If login is broken on sandbox — read this first
8	
9	**Symptoms reported by the user:**
10	- "Microsoft sign-in failed. Please try again."
11	- "Your session has expired. Please log in again." (including in incognito / after clearing cookies)
12	- 401 from any backend through the BFF proxy
13	- "The access token could not be verified."
14	
15	**Almost always the same root cause:** one or more of the BFF's `*_BACKEND_URL` env vars (`CORE_BACKEND_URL`, `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL`, `TRACKING_BACKEND_URL`, `DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL`) is set to a custom domain (`https://api.*.designflow.app`) instead of the Cloud Run `.run.app` URL. This breaks OIDC service-to-service auth. See §"`*_BACKEND_URL` must be `.run.app`, not the custom domain" below for the full explanation.
16	
17	**Has happened 3+ times.** The values drift because the Cloud Build trigger substitutions live in GCP (outside this repo) and someone keeps editing them back to the custom domains, thinking the `.run.app` URLs are wrong. They are not. See `~/.claude/projects/D--repos-dflow/memory/feedback_bff_oidc.md` for the full triage + fix playbook with gcloud commands.
18	
19	**Frontend changes cannot cause this symptom.** Do not waste cycles bisecting frontend commits.
20	
21	---
22	
23	## 1. What this is
24	
25	The BFF (Backend-for-Frontend) is a lightweight Express proxy that sits between the Angular SPA and the backend microservices. Its only job is:
26	
27	1. Accept requests from the browser at `/api/:service/*`
28	2. Attach a Google Cloud Run OIDC identity token so the backend accepts the request
29	3. Proxy the request to the correct backend service
30	4. Forward the response back
31	
32	The BFF is the only service that is `--allow-unauthenticated` from the browser's perspective. All backend services require the BFF's OIDC token.
33	
34	> **Note on "OIDC" in this repo:** OIDC here refers exclusively to Google Cloud service-to-service authentication — the BFF proving its identity to Cloud Run backend services. It has nothing to do with user login. User authentication is handled separately via Microsoft Azure AD (MSAL). These are two unrelated systems that happen to share the word "token."
35	
36	Cross-repo infrastructure standard: Designflow's current non-code runtime and
37	operating environment is tracked in
38	[u2giants/albert-standards `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md`](https://github.com/u2giants/albert-standards/blob/main/infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md).
39	If a change affects Cloud Run topology, Cloud Build/deploy behavior, domains,
40	BFF routing, secrets/env handling, storage, auth/OIDC, or database operating
41	rules, update that standards page in the same docs pass.
42	
43	---
44	
45	## 2. Repository structure
46	
47	```
48	src/server.js           App bootstrap, CORS, env validation, startup logging
49	src/serviceMap.js       Maps { core, item_master, tracking, data_sync } → env URLs
50	src/backendProxy.js     OIDC token generation + header swap (the core security logic)
51	routes/api.js           Generic proxy handler for /api/:service/*
52	routes/health.js        GET /health
53	routes/index.js         Router mounting
54	config/env.js           Loads .env.${NODE_ENV} via dotenv
55	env/
56	  .env.sandbox-albert   Albert's personal sandbox environment
57	  .env.sandbox          Shared staging/sandbox environment
58	  .env.production       Production environment
59	  .env.example          Template — copy to create a new env file
60	tests/unit/             Jest unit tests (matched by jest.config.js)
61	test/                   Legacy smoke tests — not part of Jest (separate runner)
62	```
63	
64	---
65	
66	## 3. Prime directive: custom-code boundary
67	
68	All project code lives in:
69	- `src/`
70	- `routes/`
71	- `config/`
72	- `env/`
73	
74	Do not place logic in root-level scripts or outside these directories without justification.
75	
76	---
77	
78	## 4. Core modification inventory
79	
80	No third-party files have been modified. All code is original.
81	
82	| File | Change made | Why necessary | Upgrade risk |
83	|---|---|---|---|
84	| — | — | — | — |
85	
86	---

88	## 5. Task-to-file navigation
89	
90	| Task | Files to touch | Files not to touch |
91	|---|---|---|
92	| Add a new backend service to proxy | `src/serviceMap.js` (add key + env var), `env/.env.*` files, Cloud Run trigger substitutions | `routes/api.js` (generic handler needs no change) |
93	| Change OIDC token or header logic | `src/backendProxy.js` | `routes/api.js` |
94	| Add a new route (not a service proxy) | `routes/`, `routes/index.js` | `src/serviceMap.js` |
95	| Change environment URLs | `env/.env.*` AND update Cloud Run trigger substitution variables in Cloud Console | Nothing else |
96	| Add/change CORS origins | Cloud Run `CORS_ORIGINS` env var via trigger substitution | `src/server.js` (reads it, don't hardcode) |
97	
98	---
99	
100	## 6. Important identifiers
101	
102	| Identifier | Value | Notes |
103	|---|---|---|
104	| GCP project | `lithe-breaker-323913` | All Cloud Run services live here |
105	| Region | `us-central1` | All services |
106	| BFF service (albert) | `popcre-albert-bff-sandbox` | |
107	| BFF service (sandbox) | `popcre-bff-sandbox` | |
108	| BFF service (prod) | `popcre-bff-prod` | |
109	| Core backend (albert) | `popcre-albert-backend-sandbox` | `.run.app` URL: `https://popcre-albert-backend-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` |
110	| Core backend (sandbox) | `popcre-core-sandbox` | `.run.app` URL: `https://popcre-core-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` |
111	| Core backend (prod) | `popcre-core-prod` | `.run.app` URL: `https://popcre-core-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` |
112	| BFF service account (sandbox) | `designflow-bff-invoker@lithe-breaker-323913.iam.gserviceaccount.com` | Has `roles/run.invoker` project-wide |
113	| BFF service account (prod) | `deployer@lithe-breaker-323913.iam.gserviceaccount.com` | Has `roles/run.invoker` project-wide |

115	---
116	
117	## 7. Services
118	
119	| Service | Cloud Run name | Domain | Auth |
120	|---|---|---|---|
121	| BFF (albert sandbox) | `popcre-albert-bff-sandbox` | `api.alsand.designflow.app` | `--allow-unauthenticated` (public entry point) |
122	| BFF (shared sandbox) | `popcre-bff-sandbox` | `api.sandbox.designflow.app` | `--allow-unauthenticated` |
123	| BFF (production) | `popcre-bff-prod` | `api.designflow.app` | `--allow-unauthenticated` |
124	| Core backend | see above | `api.core.*.designflow.app` (display only) | `--no-allow-unauthenticated` |
125	
126	All backends require OIDC tokens from the BFF service account. The BFF is the only public-facing proxy.
127	
128	---
129	
130	## 8. What to ignore
131	
132	```
133	node_modules/
134	test/        (legacy smoke scripts, not Jest)
135	```
136	
137	---
138	
139	## 9. Intentional quirks
140	
141	### `*_BACKEND_URL` must be `.run.app`, not the custom domain
142	
143	**Looks like:** Using `https://popcre-core-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app` instead of `https://api.core.designflow.app` — looks like a misconfiguration.
144	
145	**Actually:** `src/backendProxy.js` uses the backend URL as both the proxy target AND the OIDC token audience. Cloud Run verifies the token audience against the service's `.run.app` URL. Custom domain mappings do not work as OIDC audiences in practice, even though they appear in Cloud Run domain mapping configuration.
146	
147	**Why:** Discovered 2026-05-26 when SSO broke across all environments after BFF redeployments. Switching to `.run.app` URLs resolved it immediately.
148	
149	**Do not change because:** Switching back to custom domain URLs will cause `401 The access token could not be verified` on all backend requests through the BFF.
150	
151	---
152	
153	### `x-user-authorization` header convention
154	
155	**Looks like:** A non-standard header. Why not just use `Authorization`?
156	
157	**Actually:** The BFF puts the Google OIDC token in `Authorization` (Cloud Run reads this for auth). The user's Designflow JWT goes to `X-User-Authorization`. The backend's first middleware in `index.js` swaps it back: if `x-user-authorization` is present, it overwrites `authorization` before any route handler runs.
158	
159	**Why:** Cloud Run's auth layer reads `Authorization`. If the user JWT were there, Cloud Run would reject it (wrong signature). The OIDC token must be in `Authorization` for the Cloud Run layer; the user JWT travels in a separate header and is restored by the backend.
160	
161	**Do not change because:** Changing the header name requires a coordinated update to the backend's `index.js` swap middleware.
162	
163	---
164	
165	### `isLocalHttpBackend` skips OIDC for local URLs
166	
167	**Looks like:** Inconsistency — some requests get OIDC tokens, others don't.
168	
169	**Actually:** `src/backendProxy.js` checks `u.protocol === 'http:' && (hostname === 'localhost' || hostname === '127.0.0.1')`. If true, it skips OIDC token generation entirely and passes the original Authorization header unchanged.
170	
171	**Why:** The GCP metadata server isn't available in local development. Skipping OIDC for local HTTP backends lets developers run against a local backend without needing GCP credentials.
172	
173	**Do not change because:** Removing this check would break local development.
174	
175	---
176	
177	### Proxy hardening: timeouts, OIDC client cache, key-parse cache, fail-closed CORS (2026-06-22)
178	
179	**What changed:** four `src/` changes in one pass.
180	- `routes/api.js`: the proxy now has real timeouts (`proxyTimeout` = `BFF_PROXY_TIMEOUT_MS` (default 30000), socket `timeout` = +1000). Previously both were `0` — a hung backend held the BFF connection open forever. The `catch` around `attachInvokingIdentityHeaders` was also simplified: it can only be an identity/OIDC failure (proxy errors go to `proxy.on("error")`), so the dead `err.response` passthrough was removed and it returns a plain 500.
181	- `src/backendProxy.js`: the OIDC `IdTokenClient` is cached per target audience in a `Map` (`getIdTokenClient`). It was being re-created and the ID token re-fetched from the GCP metadata server on *every* proxied request. Rejected clients are evicted so the next request retries.
182	- `src/apiKeyAuth.js`: `DESIGNFLOW_API_KEYS` is parsed + per-entry SHA-256 hashed once and memoized (`getApiKeys`), not on every request. The config error still surfaces through the middleware's existing try/catch.
183	- `src/server.js`: CORS fails closed (see `CORS_ORIGINS` in §10).
184	
185	**Why:** the no-timeout proxy was a stability risk; the per-request OIDC fetch and key re-parse were avoidable latency/CPU; the open-CORS default (reflect any origin + credentials) was a security gap.
186	
187	**Future sessions should:** keep the OIDC client cache keyed by audience (don't cache rejected clients); if you add a new API-key write path, remember the parsed list is cached for process lifetime (restart or add invalidation). These touch BFF routing + auth/OIDC + secrets/env, so the cross-repo standard `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md` in `u2giants/albert-standards` should be updated too (NOT done this session — that repo isn't checked out locally).
188	
189	---
190	
191	## 10. Environment variables
192	
193	| Variable | Purpose | Set in Cloud Run via | Required locally |
194	|---|---|---|---|
195	| `NODE_ENV` | Selects `.env.${NODE_ENV}` file at startup | `cloudbuild.yaml` `_APP_ENV` substitution | yes |
196	| `CORE_BACKEND_URL` | Core backend `.run.app` service URL (OIDC audience + proxy target) | Cloud Build trigger `_CORE_BACKEND_URL` | yes |
197	| `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL` | Item master backend `.run.app` service URL | Cloud Build trigger | yes |
198	| `TRACKING_BACKEND_URL` | Tracking backend `.run.app` service URL | Cloud Build trigger | yes |
199	| `DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL` | Data sync backend `.run.app` service URL | Cloud Build trigger | yes |
200	| `API_ADDRESS` | Public URL of the BFF (informational, logged at startup) | Cloud Build trigger `_API_ADDRESS` | no |
201	| `CORS_ORIGINS` | Comma/pipe-separated CORS allowlist. **Fail-closed (2026-06-22):** if unset, cross-origin is allowed ONLY when `NODE_ENV=localhost`; in any other env unset = all cross-origin blocked + a startup `[WARN]`. Must be set in deployed envs. | Cloud Build trigger `_CORS_ORIGINS` | yes (non-local) |
202	| `BFF_PROXY_TIMEOUT_MS` | Upstream proxy timeout (ms), added 2026-06-22. Default `30000`; socket timeout = +1000. Replaces the old `0` (no timeout). | optional `--set-env-vars` | no |
203	| `PORT` | Express listen port | default `5004` | no |
204	
205	---
206	
207	## 11. Deployment
208	
209	```
210	git push → Cloud Build trigger
211	  → docker build (node:lts, npm install, tests must pass)
212	  → docker push → Artifact Registry
213	  → gcloud run deploy
214	```
215	
216	Cloud Build triggers are configured in Google Cloud Console (not in `cloudbuild.yaml`). Each environment has its own trigger with separate substitution variables — specifically `_CORE_BACKEND_URL` and other `*_BACKEND_URL` values must be `.run.app` URLs.
217	
218	**Rollback:**
219	```bash
220	gcloud run revisions list --service=<bff-service> --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913
221	gcloud run services update-traffic <bff-service> --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 --to-revisions=<REVISION>=100
222	```
223	
224	---
225	
226	## 12. Incidents
227	
228	### 2026-05-26 — Microsoft SSO failed due to Google Cloud OIDC audience mismatch
229	
230	**Symptom:** Microsoft sign-in appeared to succeed (popup opened and closed) but the app showed "Microsoft sign-in failed." `POST /api/core/auth/azure` returned `401`. Cloud Run logged: `The request was not authorized to invoke this service. The access token could not be verified.`
231	
232	**What broke and why:** Microsoft SSO itself was fine — Azure AD authenticated the user correctly. The failure was in the next step: the BFF proxying the user's Azure token to the backend. The BFF attaches a Google Cloud OIDC token to every backend request so Cloud Run accepts it (see §1). That Google OIDC token had the wrong `audience` claim because `CORE_BACKEND_URL` was set to the custom domain (`https://api.core.*.designflow.app`). Cloud Run validates OIDC token audiences against the service's `.run.app` URL only — custom domains are not accepted, even when they correctly route traffic to the service.
233	
234	**Why it appeared suddenly:** The BFF was redeployed (revision 00007, 2026-05-25) adding the OIDC token attachment code (`src/backendProxy.js`). Before that revision, the BFF sent no Authorization header to the backend, causing a different error (`Empty Authorization header`). The OIDC audience was never validated before because OIDC was never used before.
235	
236	**Fix:** Updated all four `*_BACKEND_URL` env vars in all three BFF Cloud Run services to `.run.app` service URLs via `gcloud run services update --update-env-vars`. Also updated `env/.env.*` files in this repo to match.
237	
238	**Commands run:**
239	```bash
240	gcloud run services update popcre-albert-bff-sandbox --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
241	  --update-env-vars "CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-backend-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-item-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-tracking-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-sync-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app"
242	
243	gcloud run services update popcre-bff-sandbox --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
244	  --update-env-vars "CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-core-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-item-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-tracking-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-sync-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app"
245	
246	gcloud run services update popcre-bff-prod --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
247	  --update-env-vars "CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-core-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-item-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-tracking-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-sync-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app"
248	```
249	
250	**Rule:** Never set `*_BACKEND_URL` to a custom domain. Always use `.run.app` service URLs.
251

---

## 🚨 If login is broken on sandbox — read this first

- "Microsoft sign-in failed. Please try again."

**Looks like:** A non-standard header. Why not just use `Authorization`?

**Actually:** The BFF puts the Google OIDC token in `Authorization` (Cloud Run reads this for auth). The user's Designflow JWT goes to `X-User-Authorization`. The backend's first middleware in `index.js` swaps it back: if `x-user-authorization` is present, it overwrites `authorization` before any route handler runs.

**Why:** Cloud Run's auth layer reads `Authorization`. If the user JWT were there, Cloud Run would reject it (wrong signature). The OIDC token must be in `Authorization` for the Cloud Run layer; the user JWT travels in a separate header and is restored by the backend.

**Do not change because:** Changing the header name requires a coordinated update to the backend's `index.js` swap middleware.

---

### `isLocalHttpBackend` skips OIDC for local URLs

**Looks like:** Inconsistency — some requests get OIDC tokens, others don't.

**Actually:** `src/backendProxy.js` checks `u.protocol === 'http:' && (hostname === 'localhost' || hostname === '127.0.0.1')`. If true, it skips OIDC token generation entirely and passes the original Authorization header unchanged.

**Why:** The GCP metadata server isn't available in local development. Skipping OIDC for local HTTP backends lets developers run against a local backend without needing GCP credentials.

**Do not change because:** Removing this check would break local development.

---

### Proxy hardening: timeouts, OIDC client cache, key-parse cache, fail-closed CORS (2026-06-22)

**What changed:** four `src/` changes in one pass.
- `routes/api.js`: the proxy now has real timeouts (`proxyTimeout` = `BFF_PROXY_TIMEOUT_MS` (default 30000), socket `timeout` = +1000). Previously both were `0` — a hung backend held the BFF connection open forever. The `catch` around `attachInvokingIdentityHeaders` was also simplified: it can only be an identity/OIDC failure (proxy errors go to `proxy.on("error")`), so the dead `err.response` passthrough was removed and it returns a plain 500.
- `src/backendProxy.js`: the OIDC `IdTokenClient` is cached per target audience in a `Map` (`getIdTokenClient`). It was being re-created and the ID token re-fetched from the GCP metadata server on *every* proxied request. Rejected clients are evicted so the next request retries.
- `src/apiKeyAuth.js`: `DESIGNFLOW_API_KEYS` is parsed + per-entry SHA-256 hashed once and memoized (`getApiKeys`), not on every request. The config error still surfaces through the middleware's existing try/catch.
- `src/server.js`: CORS fails closed (see `CORS_ORIGINS` in §10).

**Why:** the no-timeout proxy was a stability risk; the per-request OIDC fetch and key re-parse were avoidable latency/CPU; the open-CORS default (reflect any origin + credentials) was a security gap.

**Future sessions should:** keep the OIDC client cache keyed by audience (don't cache rejected clients); if you add a new API-key write path, remember the parsed list is cached for process lifetime (restart or add invalidation). These touch BFF routing + auth/OIDC + secrets/env, so the cross-repo standard `infrastructure/designflow-cloud-run.md` in `u2giants/albert-standards` should be updated too (NOT done this session — that repo isn't checked out locally).

---

## 10. Environment variables

| Variable | Purpose | Set in Cloud Run via | Required locally |
|---|---|---|---|
| `NODE_ENV` | Selects `.env.${NODE_ENV}` file at startup | `cloudbuild.yaml` `_APP_ENV` substitution | yes |
| `CORE_BACKEND_URL` | Core backend `.run.app` service URL (OIDC audience + proxy target) | Cloud Build trigger `_CORE_BACKEND_URL` | yes |
| `ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL` | Item master backend `.run.app` service URL | Cloud Build trigger | yes |
| `TRACKING_BACKEND_URL` | Tracking backend `.run.app` service URL | Cloud Build trigger | yes |
| `DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL` | Data sync backend `.run.app` service URL | Cloud Build trigger | yes |
| `API_ADDRESS` | Public URL of the BFF (informational, logged at startup) | Cloud Build trigger `_API_ADDRESS` | no |
| `CORS_ORIGINS` | Comma/pipe-separated CORS allowlist. **Fail-closed (2026-06-22):** if unset, cross-origin is allowed ONLY when `NODE_ENV=localhost`; in any other env unset = all cross-origin blocked + a startup `[WARN]`. Must be set in deployed envs. | Cloud Build trigger `_CORS_ORIGINS` | yes (non-local) |
| `BFF_PROXY_TIMEOUT_MS` | Upstream proxy timeout (ms), added 2026-06-22. Default `30000`; socket timeout = +1000. Replaces the old `0` (no timeout). | optional `--set-env-vars` | no |
| `PORT` | Express listen port | default `5004` | no |

---

## 11. Deployment

```
git push → Cloud Build trigger
  → docker build (node:lts, npm install, tests must pass)
  → docker push → Artifact Registry
  → gcloud run deploy
```

Cloud Build triggers are configured in Google Cloud Console (not in `cloudbuild.yaml`). Each environment has its own trigger with separate substitution variables — specifically `_CORE_BACKEND_URL` and other `*_BACKEND_URL` values must be `.run.app` URLs.

**Rollback:**
```bash
gcloud run revisions list --service=<bff-service> --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913
gcloud run services update-traffic <bff-service> --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 --to-revisions=<REVISION>=100
```

---

## 12. Incidents

### 2026-05-26 — Microsoft SSO failed due to Google Cloud OIDC audience mismatch

**Symptom:** Microsoft sign-in appeared to succeed (popup opened and closed) but the app showed "Microsoft sign-in failed." `POST /api/core/auth/azure` returned `401`. Cloud Run logged: `The request was not authorized to invoke this service. The access token could not be verified.`

**What broke and why:** Microsoft SSO itself was fine — Azure AD authenticated the user correctly. The failure was in the next step: the BFF proxying the user's Azure token to the backend. The BFF attaches a Google Cloud OIDC token to every backend request so Cloud Run accepts it (see §1). That Google OIDC token had the wrong `audience` claim because `CORE_BACKEND_URL` was set to the custom domain (`https://api.core.*.designflow.app`). Cloud Run validates OIDC token audiences against the service's `.run.app` URL only — custom domains are not accepted, even when they correctly route traffic to the service.

**Why it appeared suddenly:** The BFF was redeployed (revision 00007, 2026-05-25) adding the OIDC token attachment code (`src/backendProxy.js`). Before that revision, the BFF sent no Authorization header to the backend, causing a different error (`Empty Authorization header`). The OIDC audience was never validated before because OIDC was never used before.

**Fix:** Updated all four `*_BACKEND_URL` env vars in all three BFF Cloud Run services to `.run.app` service URLs via `gcloud run services update --update-env-vars`. Also updated `env/.env.*` files in this repo to match.

**Commands run:**
```bash
gcloud run services update popcre-albert-bff-sandbox --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
  --update-env-vars "CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-backend-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-item-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-tracking-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-albert-sync-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app"

gcloud run services update popcre-bff-sandbox --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
  --update-env-vars "CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-core-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-item-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-tracking-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-sync-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app"

gcloud run services update popcre-bff-prod --region=us-central1 --project=lithe-breaker-323913 \
  --update-env-vars "CORE_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-core-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,ITEM_MASTER_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-item-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,TRACKING_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-tracking-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app,DATA_SYNC_BACKEND_URL=https://popcre-sync-prod-mi7si7t62a-uc.a.run.app"
```

**Rule:** Never set `*_BACKEND_URL` to a custom domain. Always use `.run.app` service URLs.

I need to stop and surface this before writing anything, because what I found changes the conclusion — including part of my own earlier advice.

## The big finding: this is already documented, and the live config violates it

The BFF repo's `AGENTS.md` is the authoritative infra doc, and it has a 🚨 section at the very top plus a full incident writeup (2026-05-26) about **exactly this class of problem**. The documented invariant:

> **`*_BACKEND_URL` must be the `.run.app` URL, never the custom domain.** The BFF uses that URL as *both* the proxy target and the **OIDC token audience**. Cloud Run only validates audiences against the `.run.app` URL — custom domains fail with `401 The access token could not be verified`. *"Has happened 3+ times… the values drift because the trigger substitutions live in GCP."*

Your live `popcre-albert-bff-sandbox` right now has:

```
CORE_BACKEND_URL    = https://api.core.alsand.designflow.app   ← custom domain (forbidden)
ITEM_MASTER_...     = https://api.item.alsand.designflow.app   ← custom domain
TRACKING_...        = https://api.track.alsand.designflow.app  ← custom domain
DATA_SYNC_...       = https://api.sync.alsand.designflow.app   ← custom domain
```

That's the documented drift, **recurrence #4** — and the region move is what triggered it. Moving Ohio→Virginia rotated every `.run.app` URL (the suffix changed from `-uc`/us-central1 to `-uk`/us-east4), so whoever fixed up the BFF couldn't reuse the old URLs and reached for the region-stable custom domains instead.

## What this means for the fix you just applied

Creating the backend domain mappings makes the **525 go away**, but per the doc, once those certs finish provisioning and traffic actually reaches Cloud Run, you'll hit **`401` — the 2026-05-26 incident all over again**, because the OIDC audience is still a custom domain. Your developer's `--add-custom-audiences` idea is the band-aid that makes custom-domain audiences *work* — so "domain mappings + custom audiences" would limp along, but it's fighting the documented design on two fronts.

**The clean fix the docs mandate:** point the four `*_BACKEND_URL` vars at the new `-uk` run.app URLs (and update the Cloud Build trigger substitution in GCP so it doesn't drift back on next deploy):

```
CORE_BACKEND_URL    = https://popcre-albert-core-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uk.a.run.app
ITEM_MASTER_...URL  = https://popcre-albert-item-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uk.a.run.app
TRACKING_...URL     = https://popcre-albert-tracking-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uk.a.run.app
DATA_SYNC_...URL    = https://popcre-albert-sync-sandbox-mi7si7t62a-uk.a.run.app
```

Then the backend domain mappings and custom audiences aren't needed at all for BFF→backend auth.

## The docs are also stale post-migration

`AGENTS.md` §6 and `CLAUDE.md` still say **region `us-central1`**, list **`-uc`** run.app URLs, and name core as **`popcre-albert-backend-sandbox`** (live name is `popcre-albert-core-sandbox`; us-central1 now has **0** albert services — migration is complete). Those are durable facts worth correcting regardless.

Given all that, the "write the structure & design into the docs" task forks. Let me get your call:
